titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 13 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/219

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Ap. 13 My p r but for 1 big
compensation I really
think one could call
oneself an unlucky number
Leave has been stopped
from tomorrow & I had
just really begun to think
it was coming off. One day earlier & at any
rate I should have seen
something of you, tho'
probably to be recalled. I'm afraid some little
pink pink pearls may
be wasted for which the
German Service are suppose
responsible; there were
very nearly some brown
mind splashes about too
last night when I went to
bed. However both of us
are out for work, & to
earn repose later so we
mustn't grumble. I am a little afraid of
Edmund's visit to Sir E.
The former is sometimes
a little provincial in his
vision, as if the West of
England contained 1/3rd of
our population & I am
very nervous lest he may say
something to Sir E about
me with the Kindest intentions. You know exactly how
I feel about this. Richardson came back from
leave yesterday looking a
new man. It was real
joy to see him. He came
back in the General's car
from Boudergen as have he
had come to the dentist
&apparently talked of
me. I fear he may
have led the Gen' to
think I am a bit
disappointed about myself. I was but if Place does
will for the Dir I shall
be glad in the end. I am getting more recon-
siled to the vision of a
quiet life later with a
p r, with a view that
we have done our part &
need not bother about
relative results & ruvands. As a matter of fact
leave might have done us
both good & our work
too, but Richardson says
I am looking much better
-rather I fear as a result
of the expectation of
seeing a p r-. He said that his
wife had wanted to see
you. I think she is
waiting for an invita-
tion-
18 Park Avenue
Bedford. Rex's name is not in
the Gazette yet. I expect
he too is blocked.
